1: “This is the written account of Adam’s family line.” Matthew opens his Gospel with “A record of the genealogy of Jesus the Messiah” — the word is genesis, directly echoing the Genesis toledot formula. The genealogical chain that begins with Adam in God’s likeness terminates in Christ, who is the true image of God (Colossians 1:15; 2 Corinthians 4:4) and the “last Adam” (1 Corinthians 15:45). This verse is the opening of the genealogical record that leads, through Seth to Noah to Abraham to David, directly to Christ. The account of Adam’s line is the account of the family into which the Son of God will be born.
2: God created them male and female — the echo of 1:27 within the genealogical frame. The blessing of fruitfulness given to the original pair is the blessing that the Abrahamic line carries forward and that Christ fulfills in his gathering of a people from every nation (Matthew 28:19; Revelation 7:9). The “they” of creation — male and female together bearing the image — is the humanity that Christ assumes and redeems. Galatians 3:28 — “there is neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus” — is the restoration of the original unity implied in this verse.
3: Adam fathers Seth “in his own likeness, in his own image.” The contrast with 1:27 is significant: humanity was created in God’s image, but Seth is born in Adam’s image — the image now carries the distortion of sin. Every human born after the fall inherits the fallen image of Adam rather than the pure image of God. This is the condition Paul describes in 1 Corinthians 15:49: “just as we have borne the image of the earthly man” — but the verse continues — “so shall we bear the image of the heavenly man,” i.e., Christ. The solution to the distorted image is conformity to the image of the Son (Romans 8:29).
4: Adam lives 800 years after Seth’s birth and has other sons and daughters. The scale of Adam’s life spans centuries — an unimaginable expanse of time before the approaching death refrain. The theme established by this genealogy is the creeping approach of death to every life: Christ enters this genealogy as the one who “has destroyed death and has brought life and immortality to light through the gospel” (2 Timothy 1:10). The long centuries of antediluvian life make the eventual death more striking; Christ’s defeat of the final enemy (1 Corinthians 15:26) is the answer to what the genealogy announces.
5: “Altogether, Adam lived a total of 930 years, and then he died.” The death refrain that echoes through Genesis 5 is the most insistent commentary on the fall’s consequence — “you shall surely die” (Genesis 2:17). Every patriarch, however long-lived, dies. Paul’s argument in 1 Corinthians 15:21–22 is built on this: “since death came through a man, the resurrection of the dead comes also through a man. For as in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive.” The “and then he died” that haunts every genealogy entry is the problem Christ’s resurrection definitively solves.
6: Seth fathers Enosh at 105 years. Enosh’s name means “mortal” or “frail man” — the consciousness of human frailty is embedded in the name of this generation. The theme of human mortality and frailty reaches its answer in Christ who took on this mortal frailty (Hebrews 2:14 — sharing in the children’s humanity) in order to “free those who all their lives were held in slavery by their fear of death.” The name Enosh is a tacit acknowledgement of the condition Christ addresses.
7: Seth lives 807 years after Enosh’s birth. The long years between each patriarch’s son and his death mark the unhurried unfolding of the genealogy that leads to Christ. The passage of time in this genealogy is the passage of time Scripture calls “the fullness of time” — when everything was in place for God to send his Son (Galatians 4:4). The long genealogy is the long patience of God waiting for the right moment in history.
8: “And then he died.” The refrain for Seth. Each repetition of the death refrain is a data point in the argument that Christ’s resurrection will answer: “The last enemy to be destroyed is death” (1 Corinthians 15:26). The genealogy of death is the problem statement; the resurrection of Christ is the solution. These brief death notices are the repeated diagnosis that the gospel addresses.
9: Enosh fathers Kenan at 90 years. The chain continues — each link in the genealogy is a link in the chain from Adam to Christ. Luke 3:37–38 includes Kenan in the genealogy of Jesus. Every name in this list is a link in the ancestry of the one through whom God fulfills every promise made since the fall. The genealogy is the story of God’s faithfulness across the long centuries between the fall and the incarnation.
10: Enosh lives 815 years after Kenan’s birth. The extended lifespans of the antediluvian period are part of the pre-flood world that 2 Peter 3:6 distinguishes from the present age. The world before the flood had a different character; the great ages underscore both the antiquity of the line and the patience of God who preserved it across these vast spans. The God who preserved Enosh’s line for 905 years is the same God who will preserve his church until Christ returns.
11: “And then he died.” The refrain for Enosh. Three deaths named in succession — Adam, Seth, Enosh — establish that neither longevity, nor godliness (Seth’s line calls on the LORD, Genesis 4:26), nor the passing of time reverses the curse of Genesis 2:17. Christ is the one who reverses it: “death has been swallowed up in victory” (1 Corinthians 15:54; Isaiah 25:8). The swallowing-up of death is the answer to the swallowing-up of every patriarch by death that this chapter records.
12: Kenan fathers Mahalalel at 70 years. The genealogical chain continues toward the flood and beyond toward Abraham. Luke 3:37 includes Kenan in Christ’s ancestry. The names in this genealogy are not incidental — they are the family history of the Son of God. As Matthew’s genealogy opens “the genealogy of Jesus the Messiah” (Matthew 1:1), these ancient names are part of the same story, the long family tree from Adam to the one who will bear the names of God himself (Isaiah 9:6).
13: Kenan lives 840 years after Mahalalel’s birth. The vast spans of antediluvian life contrast starkly with the post-flood reduction in lifespan and ultimately with Christ’s 33 years on earth. The one who is eternal — “before Abraham was, I am” (John 8:58) — entered time as a mortal man with a brief lifespan, shorter than any of the patriarchs, to accomplish what centuries of long-lived humanity could not: the defeat of death itself.
14: “And then he died.” The refrain for Kenan. The death refrain is not monotonous — it is accumulative, building the theological case that Paul makes explicit in Romans 5:12: “death spread to all people, because all sinned.” Each death in the genealogy is evidence for Paul’s argument. The same Paul who uses this genealogical reality to argue for universal death also argues that Christ’s resurrection is the universal answer: “so in Christ all will be made alive” (1 Corinthians 15:22).
15: Mahalalel fathers Jared at 65 years. The name Mahalalel means “praise of God” — even in the genealogy of death, a name of worship persists. The praise-name amid the death refrain is a small echo of the hope that runs through the entire OT — that praise will outlast death. Revelation 5:12–13 shows the ultimate fulfillment: the redeemed singing praise to the Lamb forever. The praise embedded in this genealogy anticipates the unending praise of Christ in the new creation.
16: Mahalalel lives 830 years after Jared’s birth. The long years accumulate. The genealogy is moving toward Enoch — the single exception in the death refrain — whose translation from death foreshadows the resurrection that Christ brings. The reader who knows the genealogy begins to sense the approach of the exceptional case that breaks the pattern, as the exceptional resurrection of Christ breaks the pattern of universal death.
17: “And then he died.” The refrain for Mahalalel. The pattern holds for every patriarch: even those who live nearly a millennium die. The pattern will not be broken by longevity or by name-meaning (Mahalalel — praise of God). It is broken only by divine action: in Enoch’s case by divine translation (v24), and definitively in Christ’s case by resurrection from the dead. The relentless refrain prepares for the relief of Enoch and the final relief of Christ’s resurrection.
18: Jared fathers Enoch at 162 years. Enoch — the one who will not die — is coming. The approach of the exceptional name in the death genealogy is itself a small anticipation of the approach of the one who will definitively break the death pattern. Christ comes at the end of a genealogy much like this one (Matt 1) as the one who will break what the genealogy records. Enoch is a partial preview; Christ is the full answer.
19: Jared lives 800 years after Enoch’s birth. The years between Enoch’s birth and the next patriarch’s birth continue the long slow movement of redemptive history. The God who is patient across 800 years is the same God who, “in the fullness of time” (Galatians 4:4), sent his Son. The long patience of the genealogy is the patience of divine purpose — not slowness but deliberateness, as 2 Peter 3:9 says: “the Lord is not slow in keeping his promise, as some understand slowness.”
20: “And then he died.” The refrain for Jared. The death that holds every patriarch will not hold Christ. Jared dies; but Enoch, born to Jared, will not. The contrast within a single father-son pair — Jared dies, Enoch does not — is the pattern at the largest scale: all in Adam die; all in Christ will be made alive. The single exception in the genealogy previews the categorical reversal that Christ brings.
21: Enoch fathers Methuselah at 65 years. Methuselah’s name may mean “man of the spear” or possibly “when he dies, judgment comes” — a rabbinic tradition holds that the flood came the year Methuselah died. Whether or not this is exact, Methuselah’s extraordinary lifespan (969 years, the longest in the Bible) and his connection to the flood narrative make him a marker of God’s patience. 2 Peter 3:9 — “He is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance” — is the principle Methuselah’s long life embodies. God delays judgment for the sake of the elect.
22: “Enoch walked faithfully with God 300 years.” The language of walking with God — hithhallek ʻet-hāʾĕlōhîm — echoes the pre-fall communion of Eden and anticipates the restored fellowship Christ brings. The same phrase is used of Noah (Genesis 6:9). Walking with God is the condition Christ restores: “we walk by faith, not by sight” (2 Corinthians 5:7); “walk in the Spirit” (Galatians 5:16). The fellowship with God that Enoch enjoyed — “then he was no more, because God took him” — prefigures the unbroken communion with God that Christ secures for all who are in him (John 17:24).
23: “Altogether, Enoch lived a total of 365 years.” Enoch’s years match the days in a solar year — a detail that has attracted notice across the centuries, though its significance is disputed. The brevity of Enoch’s life relative to his contemporaries (Methuselah lived 969 years) underlines that what matters is not longevity but faithfulness. Christ’s earthly life was shorter still — roughly 33 years — yet his is the life that gives life to the world (John 6:33). Quality of communion with God, not quantity of years, is what the genealogy commends in Enoch’s case.
24: “Enoch walked faithfully with God; then he was no more, because God took him away.” The single exception in the death-genealogy. Hebrews 11:5 makes the Christological application explicit: “By faith Enoch was taken from this life, so that he did not experience death.” Enoch’s translation is the first glimpse in Scripture of life beyond death — a type of the resurrection that Christ brings and that believers share. As Enoch was “taken” (Genesis 5:24 LXX: metatithēmi), so Paul uses the cognate metaschēmatizō for the transformation at the resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:51–52). The one death-refrain that doesn’t come is the first preview of Christ’s permanent abolition of death.
25: Methuselah fathers Lamech — this Lamech is in the Sethite line, distinct from the violent Lamech of Genesis 4. The same name in different lines marks the fork in the narrative: one Lamech produces the sword-song of unlimited vengeance; this Lamech produces Noah. The God who works through ordinary, named generations in ordinary family lines is the same God who will ultimately work through Mary of Nazareth to bring his Son into the world. Redemptive history moves through family lines — God’s method is genealogy.
26: Methuselah lives 782 years after Lamech’s birth — 969 years total (v27), the longest life in the Bible. Methuselah’s great age, in Jewish tradition, marks the patience of God before the flood. 2 Peter 3:9 — “The Lord is not slow in keeping his promise, as some understand slowness. Instead he is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish” — is the principle that Methuselah’s extraordinary lifespan illustrates. The patience that delays judgment is the same patience behind the cross: Christ’s coming was precisely timed for maximum redemptive effect (Romans 5:6 — “at just the right time, Christ died for the ungodly”).
27: “And then he died.” Even Methuselah — the longest-lived human in Scripture — dies. The death refrain claims the one who lived longest. No quantity of years defeats death; only the resurrection of Christ does. “For since death came through a man, the resurrection of the dead comes also through a man” (1 Corinthians 15:21). The longest life in the genealogy still ends in death, underscoring that the solution is not more life of the same kind but a different kind of life altogether — the resurrection life that Christ is and gives (John 11:25).
28: Lamech fathers a son. The anticipation builds toward Noah’s naming in v29, where Lamech’s hope for relief from the curse is explicitly articulated. The naming-hope of v29 is the most explicitly Messianic moment in the Genesis 5 genealogy: a human voice, mid-genealogy, naming a child for the relief that only God can give. It is the cry of creation groaning for redemption (Romans 8:22) in genealogical form.
29: “He will comfort us in the labor and painful toil of our hands caused by the ground the LORD has cursed.” Lamech’s naming-hope for Noah is a type: Noah will bring relief from the curse through the flood’s reset and the post-flood covenant. But the relief is partial — the ground is still cursed after the flood. The full comfort awaits Christ. Matthew 11:28–30 — “Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest” — is the answer to Lamech’s hope. Galatians 3:13 — “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us” — is the theological answer to the specific curse Lamech names. Noah is the type; Christ is the comfort.
30: Lamech lives 595 years after Noah’s birth. The lifespan that follows the birth of the child of hope continues the genealogical chain. Each year of Lamech’s post-Noah life is a year of waiting for the promise his naming-hope expressed. The waiting of the patriarchs is the waiting of all humanity for the one Lamech named — the one who will truly comfort and relieve. Christ’s coming ends the long waiting of the genealogy.
31: “And then he died.” The death refrain claims Lamech — the father who hoped for a comforter. Even the man who named his son for relief dies before seeing the full comfort he hoped for. This is the pattern of the OT saints: “These were all commended for their faith, yet none of them received what had been promised” (Hebrews 11:39). They saw the promise from a distance and welcomed it; the full comfort they named in their children came only in Christ.
32: “After Noah was 500 years old, he became the father of Shem, Ham and Japheth.” The three sons of Noah are the ancestors of all post-flood humanity — the table of nations in Genesis 10 traces the seventy nations from this threefold origin. Christ redeems from every nation: “You are worthy to take the scroll and to open its seals, because you were slain, and with your blood you purchased for God persons from every tribe and language and people and nation” (Revelation 5:9). Shem, Ham, and Japheth are the origin of every tribe and nation from which Christ gathers his people.
